title: Quiz: NISQ Algorithm Design Principles
description: Test your understanding of NISQ device constraints, circuit depth optimization, error mitigation integration, and variational algorithm design principles

questions.question: What is the primary reason that circuit depth is the critical resource in NISQ computing?

options.text: Deeper circuits require more qubits
Each additional layer accumulates errors and approaches decoherence limits
Classical computers cannot simulate deep circuits
Quantum gates in deep circuits become entangled with each other

questions.explanation: In NISQ devices without error correction, each gate layer introduces errors that accumulate multiplicatively. Additionally, longer circuit execution times approach the coherence time (T2) limit, causing qubits to lose their quantum information before computation completes.

questions.question: What is a hardware-efficient ansatz?

options.text: A circuit that uses the minimum number of qubits possible
A parameterized circuit designed to respect the native gate set and connectivity of the target hardware
An ansatz that runs faster on classical simulators
A circuit that automatically corrects its own errors

questions.explanation: A hardware-efficient ansatz is specifically designed to match the native gates (e.g., RZ, SX, CX) and qubit connectivity of the target quantum device. This minimizes the need for gate decomposition and SWAP operations, reducing circuit depth and overall error.

questions.question: In Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE), why are circuits executed at multiple noise levels?

options.text: To average out random measurement errors
To identify which qubits are most noisy
To extrapolate the noise-free expectation value from the trend of noisy measurements
To calibrate the quantum computer before the main computation

questions.explanation: ZNE works by intentionally amplifying noise (e.g., through gate folding) at multiple scale factors, measuring the expectation value at each level, and then extrapolating backward to estimate what the value would be at zero noise. This leverages the systematic nature of noise to remove its effect.

questions.question: What is the main drawback of Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC)?

options.text: It requires knowledge of the exact quantum state
It has exponential sampling overhead that grows with circuit size
It only works for single-qubit errors
It cannot be applied to variational algorithms

questions.explanation: PEC requires sampling from quasi-probability distributions, leading to a sampling overhead (gamma) that grows multiplicatively with each gate in the circuit. For circuits with many gates, this overhead becomes exponentially large, requiring vastly more samples to achieve the same precision.

questions.question: Gate cancellation optimization works by identifying and removing adjacent gate pairs that multiply to the identity operation.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Gate cancellation identifies pairs of gates that are inverses of each other (like RZ(theta) followed by RZ(-theta)) and removes them since their combined effect is the identity. This reduces circuit depth without changing the computation.

questions.question: What causes barren plateaus in variational quantum algorithms?

options.text: Too few parameters in the ansatz
Exponentially vanishing gradients as system size increases, making optimization random
Hardware noise corrupting the gradient computation
Poor choice of classical optimizer

questions.explanation: Barren plateaus occur when the variance of cost function gradients decreases exponentially with system size: Var[gradient] ~ exp(-n). In this regime, gradients become indistinguishable from zero within sampling precision, making optimization effectively random.

questions.question: Which strategies help avoid barren plateaus in variational algorithms? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Using deeper circuits with more parameters
Identity-like parameter initialization (small values near zero)
Layer-wise training where layers are added and trained incrementally
Using local cost functions instead of global ones

questions.explanation: Strategies to avoid barren plateaus include: (b) identity-like initialization keeps the circuit close to a known state initially, (c) layer-wise training maintains meaningful gradients by training shallow circuits first, and (d) local cost functions have better gradient properties than global ones. Deeper circuits (a) actually increase barren plateau risk.

questions.question: When designing a NISQ algorithm, what determines the effective maximum circuit depth?

options.text: The number of qubits in the device
The minimum of fidelity-limited depth and coherence-limited depth
The number of parameters in the variational circuit
The speed of the classical optimizer

questions.explanation: The effective maximum depth is determined by whichever constraint is more restrictive: gate error accumulation (fidelity limit) or total circuit time exceeding coherence times (T1/T2). Both must be considered, and the smaller of the two limits determines feasibility.
